title: Rotate points (basic)
description: Given a point and a definition of a rotation, plot the rotation on a coordinate plane or identify the coordinates of the rotated point.  Rotations are about the origin and multiples of 90º.

assessment_items.question_content:
Point $R$ was rotated about the origin $(0,0)$ by $90^\circ$ clockwise.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c20854db608aa227bb5336538b3fbb26a20d59c9)

**Which figure is the image of $R$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Let's imagine a circle that passes through point $R$ with a center at the origin.

We want to find the point that would lie on that circle $90^\circ$ *clockwise* from point $R$.

A $90^\circ$ angle takes up $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of the circle.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d12d3cbec9c061b38749f59b10f2f1cf5739c3cb)

We note that point $D$ is not the same distance from the center as point $R$, so it cannot be the image.
Point $C$ is the image of point $R$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Figure $P$ was rotated about the origin $(0,0)$ by $90^\circ$ counterclockwise.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which figure is the image of $P$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Let's imagine a circle with a center at the origin that passes through one of the vertices of figure $P$.

We want to find the point that would lie on that circle $90^\circ$ *counterclockwise* from the original point.

A $90^\circ$ angle takes up $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of the circle.


[[☃ image 1]]

We rotate the rest of the vertices in the same way, then connect them.



[[☃ image 1]]
Figure $A$ is the image of figure $P$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane with 1 triangle. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 6, negative 2), (negative 2, negative 2), and (negative 3, negative 7). The x and y axes scale by one.

choices.content: ![A coordinate plane with 2 triangles. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 6, negative 2), (negative 2, negative 2), and (negative 3, negative 7). Triangle P is rotated to form triangle A. Triangle A has vertices at (2, negative 6), (2, negative 2), and (7, negative 3). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6d86dd1c166dddc3f59c5f89351b255a938fab6a)

![A coordinate plane with 2 triangles. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 6, negative 2), (negative 2, negative 2), and (negative 3, negative 7). Triangle P is rotated to form triangle B. Triangle B has vertices at (3, negative 5), (3, negative 1), and (8, negative 2). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9e34ddc83d62313e28d58cb397873a0477529139)

![A coordinate plane with 2 triangles. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 6, negative 2), (negative 2, negative 2), and (negative 3, negative 7). Triangle P is rotated to form triangle C. Triangle C has vertices at (2, negative 2), (6, negative 2), and (3, negative 7). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/855aeb384cf923f46be8f19d4b3a1f347d074f4a)

![A coordinate plane with 2 triangles. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 6, negative 2), (negative 2, negative 2), and (negative 3, negative 7). Triangle P is rotated to form triangle D. Triangle D has vertices at (2, negative 2), (6, negative 2), and (5, negative 7). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bdaea4ff78545211e07be46348558399c704627c)

assessment_items.question_content:
Figure $Q$ was rotated about the origin $(0,0)$ by $270^\circ$ counterclockwise.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which figure is the image of $Q$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Let's imagine a circle with a center at the origin that passes through one of the vertices of figure $Q$.

We want to find the point that would lie on that circle $270^\circ$ *counterclockwise* from the original point.

A $270^\circ$ angle takes up $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of the circle.


[[☃ image 1]]

We rotate the rest of the vertices in the same way, then connect them.


[[☃ image 1]]
Figure $D$ is the image of figure $Q$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane with 1 quadrilateral. Quadrilateral Q has vertices at (5, 7), (7, negative 3), (1, 0), and (negative 3, negative 2). The x and y axes scale by one.

choices.content: ![A coordinate plane with 2 quadrilaterals. Quadrilateral Q has vertices at (5, 7), (7, negative 3), (1, 0), and (negative 3, negative 2). Quadrilateral Q is rotated to form quadrilateral A. Quadrilateral A has vertices at (negative 7, 3), (negative 1, 0), (3, 2), and (negative 5, negative 7).  The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/83627b5442261aa12fb26d24e297cefb1509b4cc)

![A coordinate plane with 2 quadrilaterals. Quadrilateral Q has vertices at (5, 7), (7, negative 3), (1, 0), and (negative 3, negative 2). Quadrilateral Q is rotated to form quadrilateral B. Quadrilateral B has vertices at (negative 2, negative 3), (1, 0), (negative 3, 7), and (7, 5). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f9fdf8b4f4444ac3cd86d2a4f5766ce7e52c8321)

![A coordinate plane with 2 quadrilaterals. Quadrilateral Q has vertices at (5, 7), (7, negative 3), (1, 0), and (negative 3, negative 2). Quadrilateral Q is rotated to form quadrilateral C. Quadrilateral C has vertices at (2, negative 3), (1, 0), (3, 7), and (negative 7, 5). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f719efd6a2a86715d18575df28122fb215e9c2d8)

![A coordinate plane with 2 quadrilaterals. Quadrilateral Q has vertices at (5, 7), (7, negative 3), (1, 0), and (negative 3, negative 2). Quadrilateral Q is rotated to form quadrilateral D. Quadrilateral D has vertices at (negative 2, 3), (0, negative 1), (negative 3, negative 7), and (7, negative 5). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1eb4a7a4cc0678f9219488c7d28816bb97010ad3)

assessment_items.question_content:
Figure $P$ was rotated about the origin $(0,0)$ by $180^\circ$.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which figure is the image of $P$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Let's imagine a circle with a center at the origin that passes through one of the vertices of figure $P$.

We want to find the point that would lie on that circle $180^\circ$ from the original point.

Of course, in this case, a rotation of $180^\circ$ clockwise or counterclockwise ends in the same position.

A $180^\circ$ angle takes up $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of the circle.


[[☃ image 1]]

We rotate the rest of the vertices in the same way, then connect them.



[[☃ image 1]]
Figure $B$ is the image of figure $P$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane with 2 triangles. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 1, negative 4), (5, negative 2), and (3, negative 7). The x and y axes scale by one.

choices.content: ![A coordinate plane with 2 triangles. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 1, negative 4), (5, negative 2), and (3, negative 7). Triangle P is rotated to form triangle A. Triangle A has vertices at (0, 3), (4, 0), and (negative 2, negative 2). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf3e936e116cb14ca074460b664d4051f880c73e)

![A coordinate plane with 2 triangles. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 1, negative 4), (5, negative 2), and (3, negative 7). Triangle P is rotated to form triangle B. Triangle B has vertices at (negative 5, 2), (negative 3, 7), and (1, 4). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/171973ee5b5399b6bf9de8300407585853b40ac9)

![A coordinate plane with 2 triangles. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 1, negative 4), (5, negative 2), and (3, negative 7). Triangle P is rotated to form triangle C. Triangle C has vertices at (negative 4, 1), (negative 7, negative 3), and (negative 2, negative 5). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e3ad1758b99d3720b98521dcf9eb8c2b96633a4a)

![A coordinate plane with 2 triangles. Triangle P has vertices at (negative 1, negative 4), (5, negative 2), and (3, negative 7). Triangle P is rotated to form triangle D. Triangle D has vertices at (4, negative 1), (2, 5), and (7, 3). The x and y axes scale by one.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0d9188585e0ae17b9255cf6fb4817e6ee2a31df2)

assessment_items.question_content:
Point $Q$ was rotated about the origin $(0,0)$ by $180^\circ$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which point is the image of $Q$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's imagine a circle that passes through point $Q$ with a center at the origin.

We want to find the point that would lie on that circle $180^\circ$ from point $Q$.

Of course, in this case, a rotation of $180^\circ$ clockwise or counterclockwise ends in the same position.

A $180^\circ$ angle takes up $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of the circle.


[[☃ image 1]]

We note that point $C$ is not the same distance from the center as point $Q$, so it cannot be the image.
Point $B$ is the image of point $Q$.

options.alt: A coordinate plane with 5 closed points. The x and y axes scale by one. Point A is at (0, 4). Point B is at (4, 0). Point C is at (6, 0). Point D is at (negative 6, 0). Point Q is at (negative 4, 0).

assessment_items.question_content:
Point $P$ was rotated by $90^\circ$ clockwise about the center of rotation shown.



[[☃ image 1]]

**Which point is the image of $P$?**
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's imagine a circle that passes through point $P$ with the given center.

We want to find the point that would lie on that circle $90^\circ$ *clockwise* from point $P$.

A $90^\circ$ angle takes up $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of the circle.


[[☃ image 1]]
Point $A$ is the image of point $P$.

options.alt: 6 solid points. Point A is the bottom right hand point. Point B is above point A. Point P is the highest point. Point D is the bottom left hand point. Point C is above point D. A solid point labeled center is in the middle of the page.

assessment_items.question_content:
Figure $Q$ was rotated by $90^\circ$ counterclockwise about the center of rotation shown.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which figure is the image of $Q$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's imagine a circle with the given center that passes through one of the vertices of figure $Q$.

We want to find the point that would lie on that circle $90^\circ$ *counterclockwise* from the original point.

[[☃ image 1]]

We rotate the rest of the vertices in the same way, then connect them.


[[☃ image 1]]
Figure $D$ is the image of figure $Q$.

options.alt: A quadrilateral labeled Q and a solid point that represents the center of rotation.

choices.content: ![A quadrilateral labeled Q and a rotated copy of it, labeled quadrilateral A.  The solid point represents the center of rotation.  The quadrilateral is rotated to the left of center. One vertex of quadrilateral Q is at 8 o'clock, and the corresponding vertex of the rotated copy is at 11 o'clock.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0e2d4e8881c9ea22df1d45b8dfd5041c2c6dd0a5)

![A quadrilateral labeled Q and a flipped copy of it, labeled quadrilateral B.  The solid point represents the center of rotation.  The quadrilateral is flipped across a horizontal line. One vertex of quadrilateral Q is at 8 o'clock, and the corresponding vertex of the rotated copy is at 10 o'clock.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/675acbbbe0f0d3e568144c8ab484efdf89ebd256)

![A quadrilateral labeled Q and a flipped copy of it, labeled quadrilateral C.  The solid point represents the center of rotation.  The quadrilateral is flipped across a vertical line. One vertex of quadrilateral Q is at 8 o'clock, and the corresponding vertex of the rotated copy is at 4 o'clock.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f223744bbf447754acb76708f452f0d24d79af27)

![A quadrilateral labeled Q and a rotated copy of it, labeled quadrilateral D.  The solid point represents the center of rotation.  The quadrilateral is rotated to the right of center. One vertex of quadrilateral Q is at 8 o'clock, and the corresponding vertex of the rotated copy is at 5 o'clock.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/df6449a714add45ae7ea78063078db6ac790b0f0)

assessment_items.question_content:
Figure $R$ was rotated about the center shown by $180^\circ$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which figure is the image of $R$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Let's imagine a circle with the given center that passes through one of the vertices of figure $R$.

We want to find the point that would lie on that circle $180^\circ$ from the original point.

Of course, in this case, a rotation of $180^\circ$ clockwise or counterclockwise ends in the same position.

A $180^\circ$ angle takes up $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of the circle.


[[☃ image 1]]

We rotate the rest of the vertices in the same way, then connect them.


[[☃ image 1]]
Figure $C$ is the image of figure $R$.

options.alt: A quadrilateral labeled R and a solid point labeled center that represents the center of rotation.

choices.content: ![A quadrilateral labeled R and a rotated copy of it labeled A.  The solid point represents the center of rotation. The quadrilateral is flipped across the center. One vertex of quadrilateral R is at 4 o'clock, and the corresponding vertex of the rotated copy is at 6 o'clock.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/236b62c0770b9096b3f80daa02ba62ed8a4e58f2)

![A quadrilateral labeled R and a rotated copy of it labeled B.  The solid point represents the center of rotation. The quadrilateral is flipped across the center and rotated to the right of center. One vertex of quadrilateral R is at 4 o'clock, and the corresponding vertex of the rotated copy is at 4 o'clock.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/695fda3fc452e8d2a8989c56e3906c8984a5973d)

![A quadrilateral labeled R and a rotated copy of it labeled C.  The solid point represents the center of rotation. The quadrilateral is flipped across the center and rotated to the right of center. One vertex of quadrilateral R is at 4 o'clock, and the corresponding vertex of the rotated copy is at 10 o'clock.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e91e19d806cd1d47a62a4f9e67ab9a2cdae2b9fd)

![A quadrilateral labeled R and a rotated copy of it labeled D.  The solid point represents the center of rotation. The quadrilateral is rotated to the right of center. One vertex of quadrilateral R is at 4 o'clock, and the corresponding vertex of the rotated copy is at 9 o'clock.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5a32b68bc9ebabc2c847d321c2336440ad20bf0c)
